_note: Curated list of key GA bills. The workflow fetches vote data for each from Open States. Use summaryOverride when the API abstract is absent or too technical. Use voteOverride to manually pin a roll call number if auto-detection picks the wrong vote. IMPORTANT: session strings must match exactly how they appear in Open States — verify at openstates.org/ga/bills/ before adding a bill.

ga.summaryOverride: The bill defines the legal consequences for individuals who, without legal authority, purposely or recklessly obstruct a public passage so that it becomes impassable without 'unreasonable inconvenience or hazard' and refuse to move after an official request. A significant addition in this bill is the introduction of civil consequences. Beyond criminal fines, penalties, or restitution, a person found guilty of this offense can be held civilly liable for damages resulting from the obstruction.

ga._name: DNA Samples Required for Immigration Detainees
ga.summaryOverride: The bill requires DNA samples to be collected via oral swab or other noninvasive procedures from individuals charged with a misdemeanor or felony who are also subject to an immigration detainer notice. This requirement applies if the individual is not taken into custody by U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) within 48 hours of detention, excluding weekends and holidays. Samples must be collected by the detention facility (such as county jails, municipal facilities, or state prisons) detaining the individual or the entity supervising them.

Mandates that Georgia public schools and local school systems implement policies to restrict student access to personal electronic devices during the school day to foster a 'distraction-free education'. K-8 Students: Access must be prohibited during the school day no later than July 1, 2026. High School (Grades 9-12): Access must be prohibited no later than July 1, 2027. Local systems must adopt and revise their specific policies and procedures by January 1 of each year, beginning in 2026.

Comprehensive legislative effort to advance statewide literacy through evidence-based instruction aligned with the science of reading. The state will provide grants to fund one literacy coach per eligible school (K-3) to provide onsite, nonevaluative professional learning and side-by-side classroom support. All early literacy instruction must be focused on structured literacy and foundational skills, anchored by high-quality materials aligned with the science of reading. Local systems must annually certify that their instructional materials are high-quality and aligned with state standards.

FY 2026 state appropriations budget ($37.7 billion). Funds K–12 education, corrections, healthcare, and Hurricane Helene recovery. Includes $141 million to launch the Georgia Promise Scholarship (school voucher) program.

Georgia Promise Scholarship Act. Allows families of students in the lowest-performing 25% of public schools to receive up to $6,500 in state funds for private school tuition or homeschooling. Passed the House 91–82, largely along party lines. Program launched Fall 2025.

Reduces Georgia's flat personal income tax rate from 5.39% to 5.19%, retroactive to January 1, 2025. Sets automatic annual 0.10% cuts until the rate reaches 4.99% — continuing Georgia's multi-year shift to a flat tax structure.

Authorizes a one-time income tax rebate of approximately $1 billion — up to $500 for married couples filing jointly, $375 for head of household, and $250 for single filers. Georgia's third consecutive year returning surplus funds to taxpayers.

Amends Georgia election law: defines grounds for challenging voter eligibility (death, out-of-state registration, non-residential address), requires counties to report absentee ballot results within one hour of polls closing, and restricts non-citizens from employment in county election offices.
